content.text: Two Axes of Scale for LLM Systems
Every LLM deployment faces two independent scaling pressures: the
to fit in available VRAM, or

to handle incoming requests. Vertical scaling (adding VRAM per server) and horizontal scaling (adding servers) address these differently, and the right choice depends on which constraint you're hitting and at what scale.

attrs.alt: LLM Scaling Strategies: Vertical vs Horizontal

content.text: Vertical scaling replaces or augments the current hardware with a more capable unit: upgrading from A100 40GB to A100 80GB doubles VRAM, upgrading from A100 to H100 more than doubles both VRAM bandwidth and compute throughput. Everything stays on one physical machine (or one tightly-coupled NVLink node), so no inter-node communication is needed.
When Vertical Scaling Is the Right Choice
Model fits, throughput is the bottleneck:
If a 7B model fits on a single A100 40GB but saturates it at your serving QPS, upgrading to H100 doubles compute and bandwidth simultaneously.

A single-node H100 DGX system with 8 GPUs and NVLink has ~900 GB/s intra-node bandwidth. Adding more nodes introduces InfiniBand at ~25 GB/s effective bandwidth per collective, adding 1–5 ms per all-reduce step.
A single-node deployment is one pod, one job, one failure domain. No NCCL topology configuration, no IB driver troubleshooting.
Fine-tuning a 7B model on one H100 completes in hours. Spreading it across 8 nodes saves wall-clock time but adds days of distributed debugging risk.

The hard ceiling on vertical scaling is the number of GPUs that can be connected by NVLink on a single server: 8 GPUs for DGX H100 / H200. At that ceiling, fitting models larger than ~8 × 80 GB = 640 GB of total VRAM in FP16 (e.g., 405B parameters) requires horizontal scaling.
The VRAM needed for inference at FP16 is approximately:

A 70B model requires ~140 GB in FP16 — more than a single H100 80GB can hold. INT4 quantisation brings it to ~35 GB, enabling single-GPU serving, but at a quality cost that may be unacceptable for production.

Horizontal scaling adds nodes — each with their own GPU(s) — connected by a high-speed interconnect (InfiniBand or RoCE). The model or the request workload is distributed across these nodes. Three sub-patterns apply to LLMs:
Each transformer layer's weight matrices are split column-wise or row-wise across GPUs. Every forward pass requires an all-reduce after each attention and MLP layer. This is
parallelism that is latency-sensitive: NCCL all-reduces happen synchronously during the forward pass.
is the tensor parallel degree and
is the communication-to-compute ratio. On NVLink (intra-node), this ratio is small and TP=8 gives near-linear speedup. Across IB, the ratio grows and efficiency drops.

# vLLM: tensor parallelism across 4 GPUs on one node
from vllm import LLM

llm = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-2-70b-chat-hf",
    tensor_parallel_size=4,     # splits weight matrices across 4 GPUs
    gpu_memory_utilization=0.90,
)

# Each GPU holds 70B/4 = 17.5B parameters in fp16 ≈ 35 GB
# Total VRAM needed: 35 GB × 4 = 140 GB (fits on 4× A100 80GB)

Transformer layers are divided into stages, each assigned to a different node. Data flows through the pipeline: node 1 processes layers 0–15, node 2 processes 16–31, etc. No all-reduce between layers — only forward activations are sent to the next stage.
: while node 1 works on micro-batch 2, node 2 is idle waiting. Micro-batching reduces bubble fraction:
is the number of micro-batches. With
For inference, the simplest horizontal scaling is
: run N independent copies of the model, each handling a fraction of the request traffic. No communication between replicas. A load balancer (or Kubernetes Service) distributes requests.
linearly with N. It does not help when
one model doesn't fit on one serving unit
— for that you need tensor or pipeline parallelism.

# Kubernetes: scale vLLM replicas for throughput
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: vllm-7b-replicas
spec:
  replicas: 4             # 4 independent copies, each on 1 GPU
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: vllm-7b
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: vllm
          image: vllm/vllm-openai:latest
          args:
            - --model=meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf
            - --tensor-parallel-size=1
          resources:
            limits:
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
spec:
  selector:
    app: vllm-7b
  sessionAffinity: None
Production pre-training of very large models (70B–405B+) combines all three parallelism dimensions simultaneously — this is called
within each node, via NVLink. Width = number of GPUs per node (typically 8).
across nodes, one stage per node or group of nodes. Communication is only forward activation tensors.
across TP×PP replica groups. Each group processes a different batch shard; gradients are synchronised via all-reduce.

# Megatron-LM 3D parallelism (launch command)
# 256 GPUs = TP=8 × PP=4 × DP=8

torchrun \
  --nnodes=32 \             # 32 nodes × 8 GPUs = 256 total
  --nproc_per_node=8 \
  pretrain_gpt.py \
  --tensor-model-parallel-size 8 \    # TP within each node
  --pipeline-model-parallel-size 4 \  # PP across 4 node groups
  # DP = 256 / (8×4) = 8 (8 data-parallel replicas)
  --num-layers 96 \          # distributed as 96/4 = 24 layers per PP stage
  --hidden-size 12288 \
  --num-attention-heads 96 \
  --micro-batch-size 2 \
  --global-batch-size 1024 \          # 1024 / (2 × 8 DP replicas × 8 GA steps)
  --train-iters 300000
The fundamental cost of horizontal scaling for LLM training is
. At 70B parameters in FP32, that is 280 GB of data exchanged per all-reduce step.
Prefer PP or DP over TP
Avoid TP across nodes; use PP or DP
ZeRO: Data Parallelism with Memory Sharding
Standard data parallelism replicates the full model on every GPU, wasting VRAM.
(Zero Redundancy Optimizer) stages eliminate this by sharding optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across data-parallel ranks:

# DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 3 config
zero_config = {
    "zero_optimization": {
        "stage": 3,
        "overlap_comm": True,
        "contiguous_gradients": True,
        "sub_group_size": 1e9,
        "reduce_bucket_size": 5e8,
        "stage3_prefetch_bucket_size": 5e8,
        "stage3_param_persistence_threshold": 1e6,
        "stage3_gather_16bit_weights_on_model_save": True,
        "offload_optimizer": {
            "device": "cpu",         # offload to CPU RAM for even larger models
            "pin_memory": True,
        },
    },
    "bf16": {"enabled": True},
    "gradient_clipping": 1.0,
}

# With ZeRO-3 and CPU offload on 8× A100 80GB:
# Effective model capacity ≈ 8×80 GB (VRAM) + N×RAM (CPU offload)
# Can train ~175B model on 8 A100s at low throughput
Choose the parallelism strategy based on model size and available hardware:
Model fits on 1 GPU (≤24 GB at INT4, or ≤80 GB at FP16):
Use replica scaling only. No model parallelism needed. Scale throughput horizontally by adding more single-GPU pods.
Model fits on 1 node (≤8× 80 GB = 640 GB):
Use tensor parallelism within the node (TP=2/4/8). NVLink bandwidth makes TP efficient. vLLM's
Use pipeline parallelism (PP) across nodes with tensor parallelism within each node. This is the 3D parallelism pattern.
Add data parallelism — each pipeline replica processes a different micro-batch. Gradient synchronisation uses DP all-reduce; ZeRO-2/3 reduces gradient and parameter communication.

# Throughput benchmark across TP degrees (vLLM)
import subprocess

for tp in [1, 2, 4, 8]:
    result = subprocess.run([
        "python3", "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.benchmark_throughput",
        "--model", "meta-llama/Llama-2-70b-chat-hf",
        "--tensor-parallel-size", str(tp),
        "--num-prompts", "500",
        "--input-len", "512",
        "--output-len", "256",
        "--backend", "vllm",
    ], capture_output=True, text=True)

    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        if "Throughput" in line:
            print(f"TP={tp}: {line.strip()}")

# Typical output on 8× A100 80GB:
# TP=2: Throughput: 312.4 requests/s, 79956.7 tokens/s
# TP=4: Throughput: 418.2 requests/s, 107088.0 tokens/s  ← peak
# TP=8: Throughput: 391.7 requests/s, 100275.2 tokens/s  ← NVLink overhead shows
Scaling TP across nodes without IB:
Inter-node TP without InfiniBand (e.g., over 10 GbE) can reduce throughput compared to a single GPU. Measure all-reduce bandwidth before choosing TP>node-width.
Tensor parallelism reduces weight memory but activations are not sharded. With large batch sizes, activation memory can dominate and OOM even with TP.
PP with micro-batch=1 wastes (p-1)/p of compute. Always tune micro-batch count to at least 4× pipeline stages.
Assuming replica scaling is always cheaper:
4 replicas of a 7B model need 4 GPUs. TP=4 for a 70B model also needs 4 GPUs. The 70B model at TP=4 often has higher throughput per GPU due to larger effective batch sizes.
Stage 3 adds all-gather before each forward pass to reconstruct parameters. For small models that fit under ZeRO-2, Stage 3 reduces throughput by 15–30% vs Stage 2 for no memory benefit.
Before committing to a scaling approach, measure
— how throughput changes as you add GPUs while keeping total work fixed:
. Communication overhead reduces this. Below 0.7 (70% efficiency), adding more GPUs provides diminishing returns and the money is better spent on vertical scale or software optimisation.

# Measure strong scaling efficiency for a training workload
import subprocess, time

def benchmark_throughput(num_gpus: int, steps: int = 100) -> float:
    """Returns tokens/second for a fixed batch size across num_gpus GPUs."""
    result = subprocess.run([
        "torchrun",
        f"--nproc_per_node={num_gpus}",
        "bench_train.py",
        "--steps", str(steps),
        "--global-batch-size", "512",   # fixed — strong scaling
        "--model", "7b",
    ], capture_output=True, text=True)
    # parse "Throughput: X tok/s" from stdout
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        if "Throughput:" in line:
            return float(line.split()[1])
    return 0.0

baseline = benchmark_throughput(1)
for n in [2, 4, 8]:
    tp = benchmark_throughput(n)
    efficiency = tp / (n * baseline)
    print(f"N={n}: {tp:.0f} tok/s  efficiency={efficiency:.2%}")

# Example output:
# N=2: 1820 tok/s  efficiency=96.8%   (NVLink — near-linear)
# N=4: 3490 tok/s  efficiency=92.7%
# N=8: 6400 tok/s  efficiency=85.1%
# N=16 across 2 nodes (IB):
# N=16: 9800 tok/s  efficiency=65.2%  ← IB overhead shows

(fixed work per GPU, total batch grows with N), efficiency should remain near 100% for data parallel workloads because communication is a fixed fraction of compute per step. Weak scaling efficiency below 90% indicates a communication bottleneck that will worsen as you scale further.

Vertical scaling (better or more GPUs per node) is simpler, lower-latency, and preferable when the model fits within a single NVLink domain. Horizontal scaling (more nodes) is necessary for models exceeding single-node VRAM and for training at scale, but introduces inter-node bandwidth as a new bottleneck. 3D parallelism (TP × PP × DP) is the standard recipe for pre-training models above 70B. ZeRO stages reduce data-parallel memory overhead without changing the horizontal scale-out pattern.
